title: DAVID — Why Latin Never Really Died

gate_0.music_plan: original score only — music cleared, cue sheet on file

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Latin — the language textbooks call dead, yet priests, jurists, and scientists still read it aloud every week.
When a tongue leaves the nursery, we assume silence. Latin left the home — but never the lectern. Mispronounce it, and two millennia of text sounds like a costume.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?
The proof is continuous performance: liturgy, law, medicine — plus misspellings that betray how scribes heard vowels, and poets who counted syllables we no longer say.
We rebuild Latin corpus-first: Roman grammarians on accent, medieval Irish scribes copying classical quantities, and Renaissance humanists who argued over c and v.
Church Latin after the eighth century is attested in use — but classical vowel length is largely reconstructed from meter. I label each claim accordingly.
Listen — Gallia est omnis divisa in partes tres — hard c, v as w, ae as a single syllable in Caesar's prose rhythm.
Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
